---
title: "Lesson 4: The Invariance Property of MLE"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---

::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_the-invariance-property.mp4 >}}

[Transcript](01_the-invariance-property.en.txt)
[Handout](02_video-slides-for-the-invariance-property_module2_lesson4.pdf){width=700 height=980}
:::

## Motivation: Transforming an Estimator {#sec-transforming-estimators}

Suppose $X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n$
is a random sample from a distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.

The sample mean $\overline{X}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i$ is an unbiased estimator of $\mu$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[\overline{X}]
=
\mu.
$$

It does not follow that $\overline{X}^{\,2}$ is an unbiased estimator of $\mu^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\overline{X}^{\,2}\right]
&= \operatorname{Var}(\overline{X}) + \left(\mathbb{E}[\overline{X}]\right)^2 \\
&= \frac{\sigma^2}{n} + \mu^2
\end{aligned}
$$

::: {.callout-warning}
Unbiasedness is generally not preserved by nonlinear transformations. Maximum likelihood estimators have a different property: invariance under transformations.
:::

## Exponential Example: Estimating $\lambda^2$ {#sec-exponential-lambda-squared}

Suppose
$X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{Exp}(\operatorname{rate}=\lambda), \qquad \lambda>0.$

The density of one observation is
$f(x;\lambda) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x} \mathbf{1}_{(0,\infty)}(x)$

We want the maximum likelihood estimator of
$\tau = \lambda^2.$

### Direct reparameterization {#sec-direct-reparameterization}

Because $\lambda>0$,

$\lambda = \sqrt{\tau}.$

The density written in terms of $\tau$ is

$$
f(x;\tau)
=
\sqrt{\tau}\,
e^{-\sqrt{\tau}\,x}
\mathbf{1}_{(0,\infty)}(x).
$$

For observed data $x_1,\ldots,x_n$, the likelihood is

$$
\widetilde{L}(\tau)
=
\tau^{n/2}
\exp\left\{
-\sqrt{\tau}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i
\right\}.
$$

The log-likelihood is

$$
\widetilde{\ell}(\tau)
=
\frac{n}{2}\log\tau
-
\sqrt{\tau}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i.
$$

Differentiate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{d\tau}
\widetilde{\ell}(\tau)
&=
\frac{n}{2\tau}
-
\frac{1}{2\sqrt{\tau}}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i.
\end{aligned}
$$

Set the derivative equal to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0
&=
\frac{n}{2\tau}
-
\frac{1}{2\sqrt{\tau}}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i \\
n
&=
\sqrt{\tau}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i \\
\sqrt{\tau}
&=
\frac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i}
=
\frac{1}{\overline{x}}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\hat{\tau}
=
\frac{1}{\overline{X}^{\,2}}.
$$

### Using the estimator of $\lambda$ {#sec-plug-in-lambda-mle}

The maximum likelihood estimator of the exponential rate is

$$
\hat{\lambda}
=
\frac{1}{\overline{X}}.
$$

Hence,

$$
\hat{\tau}
=
\hat{\lambda}^{\,2}
=
\frac{1}{\overline{X}^{\,2}}.
$$

This agreement is an instance of the invariance property.

## Invariance Theorem {#sec-invariance-theorem}

::: {#thm-mle-invariance .theorem}
## Invariance property of maximum likelihood estimators

Let $\hat{\theta}$ be a maximum likelihood estimator of $\theta$, and let

$$
\tau
=
g(\theta).
$$

Then a maximum likelihood estimator of $\tau$ is

$$
\boxed{
\hat{\tau}
=
g(\hat{\theta})
}.
$$
:::

For a one-to-one transformation, the transformed likelihood is obtained by rewriting the original likelihood in the new parameter.

Suppose

$$
L(\theta)
=
\widetilde{L}\!\left(g(\theta)\right).
$$

If $g$ is invertible, then maximizing $L(\theta)$ over $\theta$ is equivalent to maximizing $\widetilde{L}(\tau)$ over $\tau=g(\theta)$. Therefore,

$$
\hat{\tau}
=
g(\hat{\theta}).
$$

### Derivative argument for an invertible transformation {#sec-invariance-derivative-proof}

Assume that:

- $g$ is differentiable and invertible;
- $g'(\theta)\neq 0$;
- the likelihood has a unique interior maximum.

By the chain rule,

$$
\frac{d}{d\theta}L(\theta)
=
\frac{d}{d\tau}
\widetilde{L}\!\left(g(\theta)\right)
g'(\theta).
$$

At $\theta=\hat{\theta}$,

$$
\frac{d}{d\theta}L(\hat{\theta})
=
0.
$$

Since $g'(\hat{\theta})\neq 0$,

$$
\left.
\frac{d}{d\tau}
\widetilde{L}(\tau)
\right|_{\tau=g(\hat{\theta})}
=
0.
$$

Thus $g(\hat{\theta})$ is the unique maximizer of the transformed likelihood:

$$
\hat{\tau}
=
g(\hat{\theta}).
$$

::: {.callout-note}
The theorem is more general than this derivative proof. The proof above treats the one-to-one, differentiable case used in the exponential example.
:::

## Poisson Example: Estimating a Probability {#sec-poisson-positive-probability}

Suppose

$$
X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda),
\qquad
\lambda\geq 0.
$$

We want to estimate the probability that a typical observation is greater than zero:

$$
p
=
P_\lambda(X_i>0).
$$

For a Poisson random variable,

$$
\begin{aligned}
p
&=
1-P_\lambda(X_i=0) \\
&=
1-\frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^0}{0!} \\
&=
1-e^{-\lambda}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus

$$
p
=
\tau(\lambda),
\qquad
\tau(\lambda)
=
1-e^{-\lambda}.
$$

## Estimator 1: The Observed Proportion Above Zero {#sec-poisson-estimator-one}

A direct estimator is

$$
\hat{p}_1
=
\frac{\#\{X_i>0\}}{n}
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\mathbf{1}_{\{X_i>0\}}.
$$

Its expectation is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\hat{p}_1]
&=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\mathbb{E}\!\left[
\mathbf{1}_{\{X_i>0\}}
\right] \\
&=
\mathbb{E}\!\left[
\mathbf{1}_{\{X_1>0\}}
\right] \\
&=
0\cdot P(X_1\leq 0)
+
1\cdot P(X_1>0) \\
&=
P(X_1>0) \\
&=
1-e^{-\lambda} \\
&=
p.
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $\hat{p}_1$ is unbiased.

## Estimator 2: The Invariance Estimator {#sec-poisson-invariance-estimator}

The Poisson probability mass function is

$$
f(x;\lambda)
=
\frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^x}{x!}
\mathbf{1}_{\{0,1,2,\ldots\}}(x).
$$

The joint probability mass function is

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\boldsymbol{x};\lambda)
&=
\prod_{i=1}^{n}
\frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^{x_i}}{x_i!}
\mathbf{1}_{\{0,1,2,\ldots\}}(x_i) \\
&=
e^{-n\lambda}
\lambda^{\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i}
\frac{1}{\prod_{i=1}^{n}x_i!}
\prod_{i=1}^{n}
\mathbf{1}_{\{0,1,2,\ldots\}}(x_i).
\end{aligned}
$$

A likelihood is

$$
L(\lambda)
=
e^{-n\lambda}
\lambda^{\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i}.
$$

The log-likelihood is

$$
\ell(\lambda)
=
-n\lambda
+
\left(
\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i
\right)
\log\lambda.
$$

Differentiate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{d\lambda}
\ell(\lambda)
&=
-n
+
\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i}{\lambda}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting this equal to zero gives

$$
\hat{\lambda}
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i
=
\overline{X}.
$$

By invariance,

$$
\boxed{
\hat{p}_2
=
1-e^{-\hat{\lambda}}
=
1-e^{-\overline{X}}
}.
$$

## Bias of the Invariance Estimator {#sec-bias-poisson-invariance-estimator}

We need

$$
\mathbb{E}[\hat{p}_2]
=
1-
\mathbb{E}\!\left[e^{-\overline{X}}\right].
$$

Several direct approaches are possible but cumbersome.

### Method 1: Sum over the complete sample space {#sec-bias-method-one}

One could write

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[e^{-\overline{X}}\right]
=
\sum_{x_1=0}^{\infty}
\cdots
\sum_{x_n=0}^{\infty}
e^{-\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i}
\frac{
e^{-n\lambda}
\lambda^{\sum_{i=1}^{n}x_i}
}{
\prod_{i=1}^{n}x_i!
}.
$$

This is correct but inconvenient.

### Method 2: Sum over the total count {#sec-bias-method-two}

Let

$$
Y
=
\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i.
$$

Since sums of independent Poisson variables are Poisson,

$$
Y
\sim
\operatorname{Poisson}(n\lambda).
$$

Then

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[e^{-\overline{X}}\right]
=
\sum_{y=0}^{\infty}
e^{-y/n}
\frac{
e^{-n\lambda}(n\lambda)^y
}{
y!
}.
$$

This is simpler, but it still requires recognizing an exponential series.

### Method 3: Find the distribution of $\overline{X}$ {#sec-bias-method-three}

Since

$$
\overline{X}
=
\frac{Y}{n},
$$

its support is

$$
0,\frac1n,\frac2n,\frac3n,\ldots
$$

and

$$
P\left(
\overline{X}=\frac{y}{n}
\right)
=
\frac{
e^{-n\lambda}(n\lambda)^y
}{
y!
},
\qquad
y=0,1,2,\ldots
$$

One could then sum

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[e^{-\overline{X}}\right]
=
\sum_{y=0}^{\infty}
e^{-y/n}
P(Y=y),
$$

which is again cumbersome.

### Method 4: Use the moment generating function {#sec-bias-method-four}

The moment generating function of

$$
Y\sim\operatorname{Poisson}(n\lambda)
$$

is

$$
M_Y(t)
=
\mathbb{E}[e^{tY}]
=
\exp\left\{
n\lambda(e^t-1)
\right\}.
$$

Since $\overline{X}=Y/n$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\!\left[e^{-\overline{X}}\right]
&=
\mathbb{E}\!\left[e^{-Y/n}\right] \\
&=
M_Y\!\left(-\frac1n\right) \\
&=
\exp\left\{
n\lambda
\left(
e^{-1/n}-1
\right)
\right\}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\boxed{
\mathbb{E}[\hat{p}_2]
=
1-
\exp\left\{
n\lambda
\left(
e^{-1/n}-1
\right)
\right\}
}.
$$

## Direction of the Bias {#sec-direction-poisson-bias}

For finite $n$,

$$
e^{-1/n}
>
1-\frac1n.
$$

Hence,

$$
n\left(e^{-1/n}-1\right)
>
-1,
$$

so

$$
\exp\left\{
n\lambda(e^{-1/n}-1)
\right\}
>
e^{-\lambda}.
$$

It follows that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\hat{p}_2]
&=
1-
\exp\left\{
n\lambda(e^{-1/n}-1)
\right\} \\
&<
1-e^{-\lambda} \\
&=
p.
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $\hat{p}_2$ has negative bias.

As $n\to\infty$,

$$
n\left(e^{-1/n}-1\right)
\longrightarrow
-1,
$$

and therefore

$$
\mathbb{E}[\hat{p}_2]
\longrightarrow
1-e^{-\lambda}
=
p.
$$

The graphical comparison in the slides shows a visible downward bias for $n=5$, while for $n=20$ the expectation of $\hat{p}_2$ is already very close to $p$.

## Summary {#sec-invariance-summary}

For

$$
p
=
1-e^{-\lambda},
$$

the two estimators are

$$
\hat{p}_1
=
\frac{1}{n}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\mathbf{1}_{\{X_i>0\}}
$$

and

$$
\hat{p}_2
=
1-e^{-\overline{X}}.
$$

Their bias properties are:

| Estimator | Construction | Bias |
|---|---|---|
| $\hat{p}_1$ | Sample proportion with $X_i>0$ | Unbiased |
| $\hat{p}_2$ | Maximum likelihood estimator by invariance | Negatively biased for finite $n$ |

::: {.callout-note}
The remaining comparison is not determined by bias alone. A biased estimator can have smaller variance, so comparing estimators naturally leads to the bias-variance trade-off and mean squared error.
:::


## Reflection
